headline: Zocks Deploys Agentic AI Across 5,000 Firms, Saving Advisors 10+ Hours Weekly as It Scales to Growth Engine
the_so_what: With agentic AI now capable of querying an entire book of business for untapped planning opportunities and triggering next-best actions in one click — deployed at scale across 5,000 firms including Osaic and Carson Group — wealth management CXOs who have not yet moved beyond point-solution AI tools risk ceding both advisor productivity and organic growth capacity to competitors who have.
summary: Zocks has deployed an agentic AI platform across more than 5,000 financial advisory firms — including Ameritas, Carson Group, Kestra Financial, and Osaic — that converts advisor-client conversations into structured data and syncs it bidirectionally with CRM, financial planning, tax, and portfolio management systems, saving advisors 10+ hours per week on administrative tasks. Beyond workflow automation, the platform now enables advisors to query their full book of business for planning gaps — such as clients missing college savings plans or approaching RMD age — and execute suggested next-best actions in a single click. The $45M Series B, co-led by Lightspeed Venture Partners and QED Investors, brings total funding to $65M and will be used to expand agentic capabilities, enterprise integrations, security, and compliance features. The round follows a $13.8M Series A closed in March 2025, reflecting rapid commercial velocity from seed stage in 2024.

key_stat.label: financial firms currently using Zocks to automate advisor workflows and generate client intelligence

capability_evidence.evidence: Zocks is live across 5,000+ financial firms, with two-way integrations across CRM, financial planning, tax, and portfolio management tools automating workflows including client onboarding, account openings, meeting prep, follow-ups, and document processing.
capability_evidence.metric: 10+ hours saved per advisor per week

The claim about 'clients with old 401(k)s held outside management' as a query example — this is mentioned in the source article but was cut off; it is present in the visible source text
The characterization of the seed stage being in '2024' — the source does not explicitly mention when the seed stage occurred
